medications.drug_name: Therapeutic anticoagulation (e.g. low-molecular-weight heparin or a direct oral anticoagulant)
medications.indication_rationale: Anticoagulation is the definitive treatment for PE, but it must be initiated in hospital after emergency assessment and imaging — it is NOT something the AI Doctor initiates via telehealth. The correct AI action is to escalate for emergency transport; the hospital team decides and administers anticoagulation. No dose is specified in the source, so none is stated here.

Reserved for massive/high-risk PE with haemodynamic instability and administered only in a hospital critical-care setting after imaging. It is never a telehealth intervention; recorded here as the correct in-hospital pathway for a large embolus, not a prescription by the AI Doctor.

Aspirin is NOT the treatment for pulmonary embolism. Reaching for aspirin reflects an anchoring error on acute coronary syndrome; it does not treat venous thromboembolism and can delay the correct anticoagulation pathway. A classic error of commission to avoid.

Oxygen is appropriate supportive care for the hypoxaemia, but it is delivered by paramedics/hospital, not something the AI Doctor can administer via telehealth. The AI's role is to escalate; oxygen is noted as part of the correct emergency pathway.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: On future long-haul flights, stand, walk the cabin and perform calf-pumping/stretching exercises every 1-2 hours, and stay well hydrated, to keep blood moving through the leg veins.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces venous stasis, the provoking factor in travel-related DVT/PE. This is prevention advice for AFTER recovery — not a substitute for the current emergency escalation.

patient_education_points.point: Your symptoms and recent long flight point to a possible blood clot that has travelled to your lungs — this needs an urgent hospital scan and cannot be assessed safely at home.
Do not stand, walk around, or rub/massage your sore calf — movement could dislodge more clot. Stay as still as possible, sitting upright, until the ambulance arrives.

The hospital will give strong blood-thinning medication and, if the clot is large, may give a medicine to actively dissolve it. Try to take slow, shallow breaths to keep the pain manageable while you wait.
This is an emergency — do not drive yourself. An ambulance is the safe way to travel because your condition can change suddenly.

follow_up_plan.interval: immediate emergency transport now; hospital admission, then respiratory/haematology review
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Airway, breathing, circulation and conscious state en route and on arrival; CTPA confirmation, right heart strain assessment, anticoagulation and disposition; longer-term VTE risk factor review and duration of anticoagulation.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any worsening breathlessness, collapse, loss of consciousness or cessation of breathing before the ambulance arrives — treat as a cardiac-arrest emergency.

must_include_items: Recognise a high-probability, time-critical pulmonary embolism / VTE emergency from the reported pattern (pleuritic chest pain + tachycardia/tachypnoea + unilateral calf swelling + recent long-haul flight + presyncope + low oxygen)
Explicitly link the swollen calf to the chest symptoms as a venous thromboembolic source
Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance to a hospital equipped for CT pulmonary angiography
Instruct the patient to stay still and sitting upright and NOT to walk, stand or massage the affected calf
Advise against self-driving to hospital and ensure the spouse stays with the patient
acceptable_alternatives: Framing the escalation as 'a possible clot in the lung that needs an urgent hospital scan' without naming a confirmed diagnosis is acceptable, provided the 000 escalation and positioning advice are given.
errors_of_omission: Failing to escalate to 000 / emergency department
Failing to connect the calf swelling to the chest symptoms (missing the DVT-PE link)
Failing to advise against walking or massaging the calf
Under-triaging to a GP appointment or watchful waiting
errors_of_commission: Recommending aspirin as if for a heart attack
Advising the patient to walk it off, mobilise, or massage the sore calf
Telling the patient to self-drive to hospital
Attributing the presentation to musculoskeletal chest pain or anxiety and reassuring the patient home
Delaying transport to obtain a D-dimer or other tests that cannot be done at home
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES immediate 000 escalation AND advice to stay still / not mobilise the calf. Under-triage below emergency transport, or advising aspirin/mobilisation, is a critical failure regardless of other content.
